serving.natural_language_portion: One standard double chicken burger serving is approximately 220 g, comprising two crispy chicken patties, a bun, spicy tomato sauce, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Tomato Double Chicken Burger — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Spicy Tomato Double Chicken Burger (cr_id G00010115). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fast-food chicken sandwich (USDA #21120) at approximately 2.5 g/100g; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on white-bread-bun dominant burger composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Tomato Double Chicken Burger. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a white-bread-bun hamburger composite (GI ~66), which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 220 g serving = 65.05 g) ÷ 100 = 42.9, rounded to 43.1 accounting for sauce carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g), failing DASH dietary thresholds
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this burger due to its high sodium content of 487 mg/100g; a single 220 g serving delivers approximately 1,071 mg sodium, exceeding 46% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio provides insufficient cardiovascular offset per DASH guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined-bun dominant carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) producing a medium GI of approximately 66 and high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this burger cautiously; the medium GI (~66) combined with a high glycemic load per serving (~43) and low fibre content (1.8 g/100g) will produce a significant post-meal glucose excursion, and wholegrain bun substitution or portion reduction is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (487 mg/100g), moderate fat (10.18 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat (~2.5 g/100g), and ultra-processed preparation method
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake of this burger; the combination of high sodium (487 mg/100g), moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g/100g estimated), and ultra-processed deep-frying preparation contributes to elevated cardiovascular risk, and consumption should be occasional with reduced-sauce and no-added-salt modifications where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (tomato, chili) in spicy tomato sauce, sodium exceeding 400 mg/100g RA threshold, and likely trace trans fat from deep-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this burger; the spicy tomato sauce contains nightshade ingredients (tomato and chili) that may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals, sodium at 487 mg/100g exceeds the RA-cautious threshold of 400 mg/100g, and deep-fried preparation may introduce pro-inflammatory trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Spicy Tomato Double Chicken Burger is a Western-style fast-food burger popular in Singapore, consisting of two layers of crispy deep-fried chicken patties stacked in a soft white-flour bun and smothered in a tangy, mildly spicy tomato-chili sauce. The texture profile combines the crunch of the fried chicken coating with the softness of the bun and the moisture of the sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — a level that raises concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Dietary fibre is low at 1.8 g/100g, contributing to a medium glycemic index (~66) and a high glycemic load per standard 220 g serving (~43), making it a food requiring caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The spicy tomato sauce introduces nightshade ingredients (tomato and chili) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis dietary management. The burger is classified as ultra-processed due to its deep-fried chicken patties and composite sauce formulation. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore fast-food outlets and is considered halal. Calcium is notably present at 116 mg/100g, likely from the bun and sauce components. Iron at 2.87 mg/100g is a positive micronutrient contribution. This record is sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010115) with high confidence.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010115) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is the only value adapted from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report it for this entry. All other macros and micronutrients are exact HPB values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite fast-food dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010115) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for an exact match as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100g) is an adapted estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fast-food chicken sandwich (USDA #21120) and is documented as such in nutrient_source. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white-bread-bun hamburger composite entry; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. The rheumatoid arthritis health context caution regarding trans fat is based on the plausibility of trace trans fat from deep-frying; no confirmed trans fat value is available from HPB for this entry.
